text: The surgeon begins the colonic splenic flexure mobilization by dissecting the inferior mesenteric vein. A peritoneal window is opened below the vein using blunt and sharp dissection techniques. Mesenteric windows are created above and below the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing the left colic artery and inferior mesenteric vein for ligation by dissecting them away from the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon performs a central tie on the left colic artery, followed by the isolation and division of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein near the ligament of Treitz junction. The colon is reflected medially, and the lateral attachments of the left colon are dissected up to the splenic flexure. The omentum is mobilized with the colon, and the lesser sac is opened through the transgastrocolic ligament. The splenocolic and pancreatocolic ligaments are divided to complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The transverse colon is retracted to reveal the colopancreatic attachments. The surgeon frees the last segment of the transverse colon mesentery from medial to lateral, just below the pancreas, completing the mobilization of the mesenteric, colic, and peritoneal components of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon identifies and divides the left branch of the middle colic artery. The division of the mesocolon is completed, and the artery of Drummond is also divided.

The patient is placed in the modified lithotomy position. The surgeon is on the right side of the patient. The cameraman stands on the left side of the surgeon and the first assistant stands on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum under the inferior mesenteric vein and performs blunt dissection to separate the space between the mesocolon and prurinal fat tissue. The lymph node dissection at the root of the inferior mesenteric vein is highlighted as important due to the lymph flow pattern of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection beneath the inferior mesenteric artery, exposing the origin of the left colic artery. A central tie is applied to the left colic artery.
The surgeon isolates and divides the inferior mesenteric vein below the inferior border of the pancreas, completing the division of this vascular structure.
The surgeon incises the lateral attachments of the colon up to the splenic flexure and then divides the frenocolic ligament.

The surgeon performs an oncologic dissection of the splenic flexure using an anterior approach. The greater omentum is divided close to the stomach, and the dissection continues leftward until the splenocolic ligament is divided. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, with the patient in the anti-Trendelenburg position and the cameraman positioned between the patient's legs using a thirty-degree camera angle.

The surgeon performs dissection of the left transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreas, dividing the pancreatic ligament. The final separation of the mesocolon from the spleen and retroperitoneum is conducted using diathermy.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic vessels and divides the left branch. A supraumbilical laparotomy incision is made through the umbilical port to remove the specimen. The marginal artery is divided in its transverse and left colonic portion. A GIA device is used to divide the large intestine, followed by a hands-on lateral-lateral colicolic anastomosis, completing the final specimen removal.
Laparoscopic resection of colon cancer at splenic flexure is technically demanding, but once anatomical knowledge and technical tips are acquired, it could be feasible as minimally invasive surgery.
